NE

Second Essay

· · 2026-01-02 ·

• The initial step to free oneself from suffering is to stop craving for the cessation of suffering. • Understanding “dukkha” requires recognizing what is necessary and what is not within one’s experience. • Pain itself is not suffering; rather, the ‘painfulness’ of pain represents suffering. • Craving (taṇha) is identified as the immediate cause of suffering, leading to dissatisfaction with experiences of pain, pleasure, or neutrality. • Unpleasant feelings lead to suffering when one craves their cessation; this craving creates a discrepancy. • Pleasant feelings become unpleasant when one desires more of them, resulting in continual discontent. • Neutral feelings lead to suffering based on lack of recognition; they are experienced differently when acknowledged. • Ultimately, suffering is caused by craving, not by the actual experience of feelings themselves.

  • Feelings play a significant role in the experience of suffering.
  • To liberate oneself from suffering, one must first learn to not crave the cessation of suffering.
  • Understanding dukkha (suffering) requires recognizing the necessary and unnecessary aspects of pain within present experiences.
  • Suffering arises from craving or taṇha, which affects how feelings are perceived.
  • In the case of unpleasant feelings, the desire to eliminate them creates suffering.
  • For pleasant feelings, the quest for more generates dissatisfaction and a sense of inadequacy.

  • This continuous craving results in the sensation of lacking, which leads to further suffering.

  • Notably, neutral feelings can be misinterpreted; they may lead to suffering when not recognized or acknowledged.
  • One’s experience of pain does not inherently cause suffering; rather, it is the persistent presence of craving that perpetuates the cycle of suffering across all types of feelings: pleasant, unpleasant, and neutral.

Longer Version

In the essay “Feelings are Suffering,” Ajahn Nyanamoli Thero explores the philosophical underpinnings of suffering and how individuals can liberate themselves from its grasp. Central to his argument is the notion that the

  • Cessation of suffering begins with a fundamental understanding of craving, or taṇha. This craving, he posits, is the root of all suffering, or dukkha,

Craving arises in relation to different types of feelings: unpleasant, pleasant, and neutral.

Thero asserts that simply experiencing pain is insufficient to understand dukkha; one must recognize what is necessary and what is not within present experiences. The distinction lies in understanding that while pain itself is inevitable, suffering is derived from craving—the desire for pain to cease or for pleasure to increase. In the case of unpleasant feelings, the craving for cessation leads to increased suffering. Conversely, with pleasant feelings, craving for more can transform enjoyment into suffering due to perceived inadequacies.

The text further elucidates the concept of neutral feelings, asserting that their recognition is vital for emotional balance. When these neutral feelings go unnoticed, they contribute to suffering, as one craves for emotional experiences themselves, leading to an unfulfilled desire for acknowledgment.

Suffering originates not from the experience of feelings—whether pleasant, unpleasant, or neutral—but rather from the craving that accompanies them.

Therefore, one’s path to liberation involves recognizing and relinquishing these cravings, which allows individuals to transcend the cycle of suffering embedded within their feelings.

Just Dhamma

A quiet place for study, reflection, and practice.
